full_text:
[Manuscript introduction notes]{.underline}

- Highlight the importance of the user-in-the-loop approach

- Highlight that it is semi-automated → The workflow takes into
 > account the sample by sample variation and the pre-clinical
 > conditions. → Addressing issues

- The samples are taken one-by-one, they are not affecting each other
 > during the celltype calling (From experience we can say that each
 > sample is slightly different, this way the differences, eg. in
 > marker expression intensity, are not skewing the data.)

- Because we use the logic table, we are not using strict cutoffs
 > ('soft logic') → There are no absolute thresholds, it is relative,
 > based on the data. This part goes either to intro or discussion.

[Manuscript result notes]{.underline}

- For speed performance we will do tests first on personal computers
 > (laptops) → Shows that it is user friendly and fast, you do not
 > need a lot of resources to run it

- Test for speed on multiple sample, large sets on the servers

- Conclusions about runtime on different sets and different hardware
 > (laptop vs. server)

- 

- We can look at the cancer cells, the cancer cell states and the
 > difference in states (for biological results) → Think what we can
 > capture from this kind of data

- No extensive benchmarking if possible!! → No direct comparison, but

d the
 > difference in states (for biological results) → Think what we can
 > capture from this kind of data

- No extensive benchmarking if possible!! → No direct comparison, but
 > mention it in the discussion → A small table in discussion where
 > we highlight differences and the different features

- One big advantage of Tribus: it does not need to be retrained on
 > different cells/tissues

- GOAL: Submit Fernando's and the Tribus paper together, using the NKI
 > data in both. We will not use the PREPOST data, but perform
 > analysis on the NKI dataset and use it as new data

- Use CyTOF dataset to show Tribus works in all technologies.

[Method notes]{.underline}

- Add plots for assessing the celltype table → marker correlation
 > plots → sample by sample

- Think if this is really needed, something which would help the user
 > to fine tune the logic table - Ziqi's marker-expression plots

- Structure of this part:

 - Dataset (reference datasets and NKI)

 - Origin of samples

 - Data structures

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- The structure of Tribus

 - SOM

 - Assign cell type names

 - Explanation of parameters included in Tribus

 - The command line / parameters for running the analysis

 - Plug-in: Napari

 - Functions

 - How to use it

[Ideas for figures]{.underline}

- A lot of Napari screenshots and UMAPs (aesthetic pictures which
 > prove reliability)

ysis

 - Plug-in: Napari

 - Functions

 - How to use it

[Ideas for figures]{.underline}

- A lot of Napari screenshots and UMAPs (aesthetic pictures which
 > prove reliability)

- Show a lot of different combinations with markers and masks, overlap
 > them

- Short videos for supplementary material (external folder, providing
 > a link for it) → Eg. for CD8 T-cells (try to do it for many
 > different types), going back and forth between the images
 > (markers) and masks, showing the accuracy by overlapping the masks
 > so the reader can see real time the concordance with markers.

**Figure 1. Overview of Tribus**

- Shows accuracy and correlation (cell type & marker heatmap), should
 > be really pretty!

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- A\) A large **overview of Tribus** (a more technical workflow can go
 > to the supplementari material), should be very graphical, already
 > showing the cohorts (eg. schematic person figure for ovarian
 > cancer, tissues)

- B\) Reliability → Large snippets of **images from Napari**, show
 > both on larger sets where Tribus uses nodes and on smaller sets
 > where we have the cell-by-cell version. link to videos (datasets
 > STELLAR and TMA), use the different kind of masks (celltype,
 > marker expression, Tribus score)

**Figure 2.**

- Technical details (runtimes, etc.), performance using a new set NKI
 > dataset

), use the different kind of masks (celltype,
 > marker expression, Tribus score)

**Figure 2.**

- Technical details (runtimes, etc.), performance using a new set NKI
 > dataset

- A\) Accuracy → **Metrics charts,** Run time...

- B\) **Representative images (from Tribus and Napari), UMAPs**

**Figure 3.**

- 

- A\) Performance (Run time...), Tribus outputs

- B\) Results → Show from Tribus's output figures and Napari result

**Supplementary figures**

- A more detailed version for Tribus and Napari (figures from this
 > day's presentation can be used as starting points)

- Diagnostic marker expression histograms

- Comparison between different round of analysis - the result keeps
 > constant

[Dataset notes]{.underline}

- We can use the TMA data to test runtimes and maybe to show
 > concordance with pathologist annotated IHC data.

- The TMA set shows Tribus's single-cell feature (Under a certain
 > threshold it skips clustering and does the annotation on a single
 > cell level)

- For STELLAR data we need to create a celltype description table →
 > Check in their available data/in the paper what kind of
 > associations, markers they have

- 

- Look for CyTOF data, even a small set is enough → It is more
 > different from CyCIF than CODEX (STELLAR uses CODEX), proves
 > Tribus's adaptability better. CyTOF is widely used, would be
 > valuable

- NKI dataset
